questions.question: A 2,000-word skill file is loaded into every agent request. What is the primary operational concern?
options: The file might contain syntax errors that crash the agent
It consumes ~2,500 tokens from the context window on every request, reducing space for conversation and tool results
Markdown files are slower to parse than JSON, causing latency spikes
The skill file needs to be compiled before the agent can use it
questions.explanation: Skill files consume tokens from the context window — a finite resource shared with conversation history, tool results, and the model's reasoning space. A 2,000-word file costs ~2,500 tokens per request. This is the primary resource tradeoff to monitor.

questions.question: Skills require a vector database or embedding service to work — the host performs semantic search to find the right skill file.
questions.explanation: False. Skills are discovered through deterministic file path lookups — no embeddings, no vector store, no semantic search. The host checks known locations (current directory, project root, ~/.claude/) and reads matching files. Zero infrastructure required.

questions.question: Your org has 30 teams using AI agents. You need consistent coding standards enforced across all projects. What's the best approach?
options: Fine-tune a custom model with your standards baked into the weights
Create org-level skill files in a shared config repo, layered with project-specific overrides — versioned and code-reviewed like any config
Add the standards to every user's system prompt manually
Build an MCP server that returns the standards on every request
questions.explanation: Org-level skill files in a shared repo give you version control, PR review, CODEOWNERS, and layering (org → project → task-specific). This mirrors how you'd manage .eslintrc or Terraform modules across teams — codified standards, reviewed changes, consistent enforcement.
